ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general lamb chop entry; chump-specific cut and grilled preparation are not separately coded. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers grilled lamb meat broadly; chump-cut specificity is not separately coded at this level.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for grilled lamb chump chop as a food product; SNOMED CT food coding is limited for prepared meat cuts.

serving.description: 1 lamb chump chop (approximately 120 g edible portion)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving is one grilled lamb chump chop weighing approximately 120 g of edible meat, providing around 359 kcal, 36 g protein, and 24 g fat.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb, leg, whole (shank half and sirloin), separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/4 inch fat, cooked, roasted — adapted to grilled chump cut; cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo lamb entries
nutrient_source.organization: Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Database; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (supplementary)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol) sourced from HPB Foodo Singapore database lamb entries as reported in the input record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat (6.0 g/100g) cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 17063 (lamb leg, lean+fat, roasted: sat fat ~7.3 g/100g) — HPB value of 6.0 g/100g is plausible for a leaner chump cut. Trans fat (0.5 g/100g) is consistent with published ruminant trans fat ranges for lamb (0.3–0.8 g/100g per USDA). Vitamin C (5.0 mg/100g) is atypical for plain grilled lamb; USDA reports ~0 mg/100g for cooked lamb — this value is flagged as uncertain and retained from HPB source pending review. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and remaining micronutrients set to -1 where not reported by HPB.

gi_source.title: N/A — Grilled Lamb Chump Chop contains negligible carbohydrates (0 g/100g); glycemic index is not applicable
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (consulted; no GI value exists for pure meat with zero carbohydrate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Grilled lamb chump chop contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g. GI is physiologically inapplicable to foods with negligible carbohydrate content. Glycemic index is recorded as 0 (numeric sentinel for not-applicable pure-protein/fat foods with zero carbohydrate) and gi_category is set to not_applicable. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition. University of Sydney GI Database consulted and confirms no GI entry for plain cooked lamb.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (57 mg/100g) with a favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (280 mg K : 57 mg Na)
hypertension.guidance: Grilled lamb chump chop is suitable for hypertension management given its low sodium content of 57 mg/100g and meaningful potassium of 280 mg/100g; avoid adding salt during preparation and monitor sodium in accompanying sauces or condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar content; high protein (30 g/100g) supports satiety without glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Grilled lamb chump chop produces no glycaemic response due to its zero carbohydrate content and is appropriate for Type 2 Diabetes meal plans; however, total saturated fat intake should be monitored given the 6 g/100g saturated fat content, and portion size of any carbohydrate-containing side dishes must be controlled.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6 g/100g and ruminant trans fat 0.5 g/100g raise LDL-cholesterol risk; cholesterol 89 mg/100g is moderate
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit grilled lamb chump chop to occasional consumption (no more than 1–2 times per week) due to its saturated fat (6 g/100g) and ruminant trans fat (0.5 g/100g) content, which can elevate LDL cholesterol; trimming visible external fat before grilling and pairing with high-fibre vegetables can partially mitigate cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat arachidonic acid load, saturated fat exceeding 5 g/100g RA threshold, and ruminant trans fat (0.5 g/100g) are all pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Grilled lamb chump chop is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its arachidonic acid content (red meat), saturated fat of 6 g/100g (above the 5 g/100g RA threshold), and ruminant trans fat of 0.5 g/100g, all of which amplify inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6); substituting with oily fish or skinless chicken is strongly advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled Lamb Chump Chop is a cut of lamb taken from the rear chump portion of the carcass, located between the loin and the leg. It is a boneless or near-boneless cut comprising lean red muscle meat with both intramuscular and external fat attached. When grilled over direct heat, the exterior develops a slightly charred crust while the interior remains tender and juicy. The flavour is distinctively rich and gamey, characteristic of lamb, with a savoury depth enhanced by the Maillard reaction during grilling. Per 100 g, it provides 299 kcal, 30 g protein, 20 g fat (of which 6 g is saturated fat and 0.5 g is ruminant trans fat), 0 g carbohydrate, 57 mg sodium, 280 mg potassium, 10 mg calcium, 2.55 mg iron, and 89 mg cholesterol. The zero carbohydrate content makes it glycaemically inert and suitable for low-carbohydrate, ketogenic, and Type 2 Diabetes meal plans. Its high protein density (30 g/100g) supports muscle maintenance and satiety. The moderate saturated fat and ruminant trans fat content warrant caution for cardiovascular disease patients. The food is halal-certified when sourced from approved abattoirs. It is commonly served in Western, Middle Eastern, and Mamak-style restaurant contexts in Singapore, typically accompanied by rice, salad, or flatbread. Iron content of 2.55 mg/100g contributes meaningfully to daily iron requirements, particularly relevant for individuals at risk of iron-deficiency anaemia.

confidence_reason: Primary macronutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol) sourced from HPB Foodo Singapore database with confidence 0.95 as reported in the input record. Saturated fat and trans fat values are consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for comparable lamb cuts. Vitamin C value of 5.0 mg/100g is flagged as uncertain (atypical for plain grilled lamb) but retained from the HPB source. Overall confidence is high given the primary HPB source coverage of core macros.
notes: 1. ZERO-MACRO JUSTIFICATION: carbohydrate_g is naturally zero — lamb is a pure animal protein and fat food with no carbohydrate content. sugar_g is naturally zero by the same basis. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as dietary fibre is absent in animal muscle tissue. Confirmed zero per HPB Foodo Singapore database. 2. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and mineral micronutrients for this specific grilled lamb chump chop preparation are not separately reported in the HPB Foodo Singapore database entry used as the primary source. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 17063 (lamb leg, lean+fat, roasted) was consulted as a supplementary reference but values were not directly mapped due to cut and preparation differences. 3. VITAMIN C FLAG: vitamin_c_mg of 5.0 mg/100g is atypical for plain grilled lamb (USDA reports ~0 mg/100g for cooked lamb cuts). This value is retained from the HPB source but flagged for review — it may reflect a marinade component or a data entry anomaly in the source record. 4. TRANS FAT NOTE: trans_fat_g of 0.5 g/100g represents ruminant trans fat (primarily vaccenic acid and conjugated linoleic acid), which is naturally occurring in ruminant animals and distinct from industrially produced trans fats. This level is consistent with published ranges for lamb (0.3–0.8 g/100g). 5. CUISINE CLASSIFICATION: The input record classifies this food under 'mamak' cuisine; however, grilled lamb chump chop is a Western/International preparation that appears on Mamak restaurant menus in Singapore. The cuisine field reflects the broader Western/International origin of the dish preparation. 6. GI: glycemic_index is set to 0 (numeric sentinel) and gi_category is not_applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0 g/100g); glycemic index is physiologically inapplicable to foods with zero carbohydrate content. The value 0 is used as the required numeric type per the scoring model; it does not imply a measured GI of zero but rather that GI measurement is not applicable for this food. 7. PORTION SOURCE: The input portion of 300 g (2–3 chops with side dish) was sourced from GrabFood, which is a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2. The serving size has been revised to 120 g (one chump chop, edible portion) based on standard culinary references for a single lamb chop serving.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Grilled Lamb Chump Chop record passes all 6 hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1–Q8 scorecard. The sole failure from iteration 2 (q4: glycemic_index must be numeric) has been resolved by setting glycemic_index to 0 as a numeric sentinel value, with gi_category 'not_applicable' and full documentation in notes and gi_source clarifying that this represents physiological non-applicability rather than a measured GI of zero. Primary nutrient data sourced from HPB Foodo Singapore database (confidence 0.95) with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as supplementary cross-reference. Zero carbohydrate, sugar, and dietary fibre are justified as naturally zero for animal muscle meat. All three mandatory health conditions (Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease) are present with specific clinical guidance. Ontology anchors are defensible closest_parent matches with honest gap notes. Aliases are comprehensive and internally consistent.
